speaker: interviewer
text: --four of the interview with Rachel Kessler, on July 29, 1996. Mrs. Kessler, can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photograph of myself, I think in 1923. I had taken my finals in college, in the University of London, in 1922. And I believe that this was my cap and gown at the ceremonial-- ceremony, which must have been in 1923.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it taken in-- I guess it was taken in London. Because I lived in Nottingham, but I had to take the train to London for the examination. And probably, also, for the ceremony of-- for the ceremony finally-- finally getting my degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, Mrs. Kessler, can you describe what-- what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is my old copy of Hatikvah, that I used when I was teaching children in the cheder of my hometown in Nottingham. And I see that there is a modification later that I put-- there is a modification in the text itself. I was teaching a class for several years. Each year I would have a new class of 5-year-old little children that I taught a lot of things to. They learned all the Brachot, they learned the Shamar. They learned a lot of things. But in particular, during that one year, coming for an hour five times a week, they learned how to read Hebrew. And then when they turned six, they went to the next class. And they began reading and translating siddur. That was the system in my congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about when would this have been used?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- one of them was-- well, I was teaching, let's say, from the age of 16. That was from 1918 to 1924.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like a drink out of my glass. I would like a drink of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photograph first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my husband and I getting married. September the 14th, 1924. A very handsome young man. And well, I don't know whether you'd call her a pretty girl. I've seen pretty photographs than that. But that was done in the congregational shul of Nottingham, England. And I can still see how crowded it was because everybody was very, very curious, and come out and see one of the girls of the congregation getting married to a foreign young man who spoke only Yiddish to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Can you describe what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a telegram received in Nottingham, at the time of our wedding. It is in Hungarian and says, much loss-- luck and happiness from-- to Martin and [? Relly, ?] from your parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this would have been in what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Kessler, who-- who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Rabbi Martin Kessler, at a time when he was fairly young, wasn't he? Well, at a time when the rabbis did wear tall caps. And now they just wear a yarmulke. But that was the custom at that time. So I can't fix it, exactly. Except that it is nice to have, because it was the old-fashioned way. And we like to keep some old-fashioned things.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our son, Jack, Jack Alexander Kessler. The Jack is for Martin's father. And the Alexander is for my father. He was being bar mitzvah that day. And he's now over 50 and has given us four grandsons. He went to college. Then he went to the seminary for four years and became an accomplished cantor, chazan. He was in several congregations, but decided he would like to go in a different direction and be independent. He has a very, very good musical group and lives in Philadelphia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would this photograph had been taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, he was born in-- '44-- '57, 1957, this.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's married now. We have lovely children, and they keep in touch with us all the time. And our grandchildren keep in touch with us. We're really blessed to have children that are so loving. He-- he calls us nearly every day. He can't come often to see us, because he's very busy. And just today he's doing a big wedding, a five-hour problem-- program he has yesterday. The-- the rabbi of the congregation of where he's going is conducting the ceremony of the wedding. But then he's in charge for five years of music, with all-- klezmer, they do, of course, and the other two. He has a good group, and the group, that they get along very fine, very well. They have a good time together. And it's hard work, but he enjoys doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. OK, can you tell us who's in this photograph and what it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just a celebration of our 50th wedding anniversary. Obviously I'm there, and so is my husband. The little girl in front is a very big girl now, Suzie. Suzanne is a very accomplished artist and manages an art studio in Albuquerque. The-- the young lady opposite holding-- clapping her hands is our daughter, Ann, who is the little girl's mother. And the others are just friends of the family who came to celebrate with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: September, 1974. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] --for the 70th, we had it in shul, we had a big celebrate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And let's-- OK, can you describe what-- who's in this photograph and what it is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that happy couple, Martin and Rachel Kessler, seated in such a way that you can see some of Martin's beautiful sculpture behind us, in 1973.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a wonderful sculptor. He's a wonderful man, many gifts. He's a blessing on Earth. He's a Lamed Vavnik. He's one of the 36 angels on earth who are going to help us to get the Messiah to come. Bless him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in the 1990s? Or-- no?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1973, he was 76 years old at that time. He was 76 there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kessler, can you describe this piece?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you'd have to ask Martin what the wood is, because I'm not proficient in remembering the different kinds of wood. It may be of Brazilian Rosewood, but I'm not sure. He did this quite some time ago. I think really, it's one of the earlier pieces he did. And of course, it's very meaningful. Because usually it's been hidden in a corner. But just recently, we brought it out to stand where it is available-- visible. We have nothing to be ashamed of. Not we at all. And I do think it's beautiful wood. And if you approach it and stroke it, the-- the finish is like satin. It's so beautifully finished. That meant that there was hours and hours of rubbing down with special papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you introduce yourself and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, this is my husband, Martin Kessler. I'm his wife, Rachel Kessler. We met-- ooh-- 70-- 76 years ago--

speaker: interviewee
text: 78 years ago in the kosher student's restaurant near the Sorbonne, in Paris. We have been married since 1924, which means--

speaker: interviewee
text: That will be 72 years on September 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin will be 99 on the 4th of September. I became 94 in May, this year. We have a daughter, Ann, born in France, who just got her doctorate in French and Spanish. We have a son, a very fine son, a chazan. And we have four grandchildren. How many grand--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it like to have come through so much together?

speaker: interviewer
text: What else shall I say? We have fun together. We had a wonderful winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody complained of how much snow there was. It was a terrible winter, they say. We had one of the best winters of our lives. Because we sit there. We can see out the beautiful scene with the snow. It's warm in the house. We didn't go out. We had interesting things to read, although Martin doesn't see well anymore, I read to him. We have some really interesting things and satisfying things to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: She read the law to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's joking. You've better watch out with him, because he's a great joker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This piece is made of rosewood imported from Brazil. The letters are handmade, cut out from a plaque of brass 1/2-inch thick. Filed and polished and mounted so that there is no trace of the montage. It's flat and silvery velvety, a nice piece. And let mankind once in a while remember the 10 Commandments. This is my best work. Made of myrtlewood, imported from--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Oregon. Here, waves indicate water, the water of the sea. And two branches rising, representing life, mineral, and human. And all of it striving Heavenward.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let someone else put it back, will you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin, let someone else do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am here. Oh, I was on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can point and show it without taking it down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just point your fingers, OK, honey?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't have a little stick.

speaker: interviewee
text: This space represents the Burning Bush, made of two different woods. One is already seared by the flame. And the other is still intact. It's a very heavy piece. I'm not lifting it off. And the bush [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] remains, still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not consumed. OK, what's the next one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at the next one, Martin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next one. This is one piece of walnut from here, down, from the base up. And I made, also, the-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] candleholders out of brass. They're not exactly handmade, although these two hands fashioned it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a lathe for doing it. What did I show [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] this is a very prayerful piece. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] 'O Lord, give me a pure heart.' It's quoted from somebody reads it off. I-- I can't remember-- But you can't read it. I know. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Here is another piece and made of a single piece of what are you? This is Myrtlewood. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only piece that is not made for specific noble wood. But it comes from a piece of Northern Pine from Norway. And well, it's a good looker, I think. [LAUGHTER] It's very graceful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your two Egyptian he has two Egyptian cats there. That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's-- that's wonderful. That's fine. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one nearest me is the one that--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you describe that one?

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- OK, I'll get that. Why don't you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This here is made of five pieces of Rosewood from Brazil. And it is assembled by very heavy brass screws. And it has a light in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's on. So is on. I put the light on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a Ner tamid with a light in it. It doesn't show in the daytime, especially against that light. So I'm closing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Thank you very--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is one more piece that I want to show.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm showing this piece because it was a particularly difficult one. It represents the surf--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the sea and the mermaid looking behind the surf. This Little Mermaid has everything a mermaid ought to have. And some of it, we don't speak. It's That was difficult. made-- It's made-- made of olive wood, imported from Brazil-- from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Olive wood from Israel. Well, we can put it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: When? When did you carve that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? I-- I-- I'll tell you. When I had the wood from Israel for the last time. That means about five years ago. From then on, I no longer had any wood from Israel. It is mounted on a piece of Lucite, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which evokes water, because it's transparent like water, or translucent to-- to the minimum. And it's all one piece of wood. You try it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much, making me a little [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you look at the camera for just one more moment?
